Healthcare Needs Report

Sudeep Santhosh

library(lme4)
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(haven)
library(labelled)
library(sjlabelled)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(sjPlot)
library(naniar)
library(tinytable)
library(ggmosaic)
options(tinytable_tt_digits = 3)
options(tinytable_latex_placement = "H")

Data loading

The .sav format was selected for data loading because it preserves variable labels, data types, and metadata more effectively than flat file formats such as .csv. This is particularly useful for analytically work.

healthy_wave=haven::read_sav(here("data","CRONOS3 Theme - Make it Healthy.sav"))
dictionary=labelled::generate_dictionary(healthy_wave)

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

Duplicate value

sum(duplicated(healthy_wave$idno))
## [1] 1431
healthy_wave$needed_any=ifelse(
  rowSums(healthy_wave[, c("w1hq1", "w1hq3", "w1hq6", "w1hq11")] == 1, na.rm = TRUE) > 0,
  1, 0
)
healthy_wave %>%
  filter(idno==25213)|>
  select(cntry,age,idno)|>
  haven::as_factor() |>
  tt()
cntry age idno
Czechia 74 25213
Finland 70 25213

Checking id-25213

In healthy_wave1, 25213 id has two entries with countries as CZ and FI.

Trying to see how the ids are filled, realized that for every country there is a given range of values assigned.

So as to create unique values, we will be merging cntry and idno to create a new value, which will be the new primary key for filtering.

Solution-1 solving duplicates by merging the cntry and idno column

primary_health_wave=healthy_wave|>
  mutate(new_id=paste(cntry,idno,sep= "_"))
sum(duplicated(primary_health_wave$new_id))
## [1] 21

still 21 duplicate values exist so we merge another column which is age to create a primary key in the dataset also to check if its not just the same person and not at different ages / years we had to check this

primary_health_wave|>
  filter(duplicated(new_id) | duplicated(new_id,fromLast=TRUE)) |>
  arrange(new_id)|>
  select(cntry, new_id,age,gndr)|>
  haven::as_factor()|>
  slice_head(n=12)|>
  tt()
cntry new_id age gndr
Austria AT_56678 61 Male
Austria AT_56678 75 Female
United Kingdom GB_50416 58 Male
United Kingdom GB_50416 48 Male
United Kingdom GB_57250 46 Female
United Kingdom GB_57250 83 Male
United Kingdom GB_58627 40 Male
United Kingdom GB_58627 42 Male
United Kingdom GB_60237 49 Male
United Kingdom GB_60237 46 Male
United Kingdom GB_64252 72 Female
United Kingdom GB_64252 61 Female

from this we can say that none of them are the same person as there huge again difference gap of more than one year

on merging with with three columns which is age ,idno and cntry we were able to remove the duplicates so now we can use this column as a primary key for identifying an individual

primary_health_wave=primary_health_wave|>
  mutate(new_id=paste(cntry,idno,age, sep="_"))
sum(duplicated(primary_health_wave$new_id))
## [1] 0

Model parametrs at intial phase

no of people who Needed to see a general practitioner last 12 months but could not

sum(healthy_wave$w1hq1 ==1,na.rm = TRUE)
## [1] 426

no fo people who Needed to see dentist last 12 months but could not

sum(healthy_wave$w1hq6 ==1,na.rm = TRUE)
## [1] 472

no of people Needed to see medical specialists but could not

sum(healthy_wave$w1hq3 == 1, na.rm = TRUE)
## [1] 324
length(healthy_wave$idno[
  which(healthy_wave$w1hq6==1|
        healthy_wave$w1hq3==1|
        healthy_wave$w1hq1==1|
        healthy_wave$w1hq11==1)
])
## [1] 1048

we have increased the number of observation to 1048

Variable Description Table

Variable Role Description
w1hq1 Response Needed to see a general practitioner in the last 12 months
w1hq3 Response Needed to see medical specialists in the last 12 months
w1hq6 Response Needed to see dentist in the last 12 months
w1hq11 Response Needed diagnostic tests or scans prescribed by a doctor in the last 12 months
w1hq5 Predictor Wait time for medical specialist appointment
cntry Predictor Country
age Predictor Age
gndr Predictor Gender
w3hq77 Predictor Currently in paid work
w3hq57 Predictor Subjective mental health in general
w3hq58 Predictor Subjective physical health in general
w3hq87 Predictor Difficulty taking an hour off for personal/family matters during work
w5hq18 Predictor Healthcare costs covered by system
w5hq11 Predictor Receive help and support from close contacts when needed
w5hq8 Predictor Severe financial difficulties in family during first 18 years of life
w5hq17 Predictor Out-of-pocket cost for most recent private mental health consultation
w5hq12 Predictor Felt lonely during the past week

Country wise analysis

country_summary=primary_health_wave|>
  group_by(cntry)|>
  summarise(
    total_people=n(),
    people_needed=sum(needed_any, na.rm = TRUE),
    proportion=people_needed/total_people
  )|>
  haven::as_factor() |>
  tt()
country_summary
cntry total_people people_needed proportion
Austria 1040 50 0.0481
Belgium 1294 39 0.0301
Czechia 944 79 0.0837
Finland 1817 165 0.0908
France 1209 92 0.0761
United Kingdom 1370 184 0.1343
Hungary 757 64 0.0845
Iceland 1141 83 0.0727
Poland 645 83 0.1287
Portugal 779 101 0.1297
Slovenia 1151 108 0.0938 
ggplot(primary_health_wave)+
  geom_mosaic(aes(x=product(needed_any,cntry),
                  fill=needed_any))+
  scale_x_productlist("Country") +
  scale_y_productlist("Medical Care Status") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("orange", "blue"),
    name = "Medical Care Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank()
  )

The mosaic plot suggests that, across all countries, most respondents reported having their medical care needs met. However, the proportion of unmet health care need varies by country, with relatively higher shares observed in countries such as GB(United Kingdom), PL(Poland), PT(Portugal), and FI(Finland). This indicates potential cross-country differences in access to or delivery of medical care services.

country_summary1=primary_health_wave|>
  group_by(cntry)|>
  summarise(
    people_needed=sum(needed_any == "Unmet Health Care", na.rm = TRUE),
  )|>
  arrange(desc(people_needed))|>
  haven::as_factor() |>
  tt()
  

country_summary1
cntry people_needed
United Kingdom 184
Finland 165
Slovenia 108
Portugal 101
France 92
Iceland 83
Poland 83
Czechia 79
Hungary 64
Austria 50
Belgium 39 
service_country=primary_health_wave|>
  group_by(cntry)|>
  summarise(
    GP=sum(w1hq1==1,na.rm=TRUE),
    Specialist=sum(w1hq3==1,na.rm=TRUE),
    Dentist=sum(w1hq6==1,na.rm=TRUE),
    Diagnostic_Test=sum(w1hq11==1,na.rm=TRUE))|>
  pivot_longer(
    cols=-cntry,
    names_to="Service",
    values_to="Count")
ggplot(service_country,aes(x=reorder(cntry,-Count),y=Count,fill=Service))+
  geom_bar(stat="identity")+
  labs(title="Unmet Healthcare Services by Country",
    x="Country",
    y="Count")+
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(axis.text.x=element_text(angle=45,hjust=1))

Variable Analysis thorugh likert charts

Variable Description
w3hq57 Subjective mental health in general
w3hq58 Subjective physical health in general
w5hq12 Felt lonely, how much of the time in the past week 
label_summary <- tibble(
  Variable = c(
    "Subjective mental health in general",
    "Subjective physical health in general",
    "Felt lonely, how much of the time past week"
  ),
  Labels = c(
    paste(names(attr(healthy_wave$w3hq57, "labels")), collapse = ", "),
    paste(names(attr(healthy_wave$w3hq58, "labels")), collapse = ", "),
    paste(names(attr(healthy_wave$w5hq12, "labels")), collapse = ", ")
  )
)

tt(label_summary)
Variable Labels
Subjective mental health in general Very good, Good, Fair, Bad, Very bad, No answer
Subjective physical health in general Very good, Good, Fair, Bad, Very bad, No answer
Felt lonely, how much of the time past week None or almost none of the time, Some of the time, Most of the time, All or almost all of the time, No answer

The labels Very Positive, Positive, Decent, Negative, and Very Negative were chosen to create a consistent ordinal framework across the three well being variables: loneliness, subjective mental health, and subjective physical health. Although the original survey items use slightly different response wording (for example, frequency-based responses for loneliness and quality-based responses for health status), recoding them into a common directional scale improves the comparability and interpret ability of the Likert chart.

primary_health_wave$lonely=factor(primary_health_wave$w5hq12,levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4),labels = c("Very Positive","Positive","Decent","Negative" ),ordered = TRUE)

primary_health_wave$mental_state=factor(primary_health_wave$w3hq57,levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4,5),labels = c("Very Positive","Positive","Decent","Negative","Very Negative"),ordered = TRUE)
primary_health_wave$physical_state=factor(primary_health_wave$w3hq58,levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4,5),labels = c("Very Positive","Positive","Decent","Negative","Very Negative"),ordered = TRUE)

df_yes=primary_health_wave[primary_health_wave$needed_any== "Unmet Health Care", ]
df_no=primary_health_wave[primary_health_wave$needed_any=="Met Health Care", ]
plot_likert(df_yes[,c("mental_state", "physical_state","lonely")],
grid.range = c(1.2, 1.4),
expand.grid = FALSE,
values = "sum.outside",
show.prc.sign = TRUE,geom.colors = "RdBu")

plot_likert(df_no[,c("mental_state", "physical_state","lonely")],
grid.range = c(1.2, 1.4),
expand.grid = FALSE,
values = "sum.outside",
show.prc.sign = TRUE,geom.colors = "RdBu")

From this we can say that mental state of people who did receive medical care they had bad mental state as well as a bad physical state which makes it a good parameter for the model, we can also say loneliness is kind of high for people who need health care

Missing Data Analysis

Variable Code Description
w3hq87 How difficult to take an hour off for personal/family matters during work
w5hq18 Healthcare costs covered by system
w5hq11 Receive help and support from close contacts when needed
w5hq8 Severe financial difficulties in family during first 18 years of life, how often
w5hq17 Out-of-pocket cost for most recent private mental health consultation
w5hq12 Felt lonely, how much of the time past week
w3hq77 Currently in paid work 
missing_raw=miss_var_summary(
  df_yes[, c("w3hq87", "w5hq18", "w5hq11", "w5hq8", "w5hq17", "w5hq12","w3hq77")]
)
missing_table=tibble(
  Variable=c(
    "Time off work difficulty",
    "Healthcare cost coverage",
    "Social support",
    "Childhood financial difficulty",
    "Mental health consultation cost",
    "Loneliness",
    "Currently in paid work"
  ),
  Missing_Count=as.numeric(missing_raw$n_miss),
  Missing_Percentage=as.numeric(missing_raw$pct_miss)
)
tt(missing_table)
Variable Missing_Count Missing_Percentage
Time off work difficulty 908 86.6
Healthcare cost coverage 531 50.7
Social support 217 20.7
Childhood financial difficulty 215 20.5
Mental health consultation cost 213 20.3
Loneliness 213 20.3
Currently in paid work 202 19.3

we might need to remove two varible for the model to take btter resutls w5hq17- Out-of-pocket cost for most recent private mental health consultation -86.6% w3hq87- How difficult to take an hour off for personal/family matters during work -50.7% as they very high missing values so its better to remove them

gg_miss_upset(primary_health_wave[,c("w3hq87", "w5hq18","w5hq11","w5hq8","w5hq17","w5hq12","w3hq77")])

removing both those column remove a high chunk of missing values as we can see w5hq17 and a combination of both w3hq87 with w5hq17 have the most missing values

gg_miss_case(primary_health_wave[,c("w3hq87", "w5hq18","w5hq11","w5hq8","w5hq17","w5hq12","w3hq77")])

comparing the miss cases on removing the two columns

gg_miss_case(primary_health_wave[,c( "w5hq18","w5hq11","w5hq8","w5hq12","w3hq77")])

ggplot(primary_health_wave,aes(x=factor(needed_any),y=age,fill=factor(gndr)))+
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA)+ 
  labs(x="Healthcare Need Status",
    y="Age",
    title="Age Distribution by Healthcare Need Status")+
  scale_x_discrete(labels=c("Met","Unmet"))+
  scale_fill_manual(name="Gender",
    labels=c("Male", "Female","No answer"),
    values=c("blue","pink","black")
  )+facet_wrap(vars(cntry))

primary_health_wave |>
  filter(!is.na(gndr))|>
  group_by(cntry, needed_any, gndr)|>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    median_age = median(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    age_range = paste(
      quantile(age, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
      " - ",
      quantile(age, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
    ),.groups = "drop")|>
  haven::as_factor() |>
  tt()
cntry needed_any gndr n median_age age_range
Austria Met Health Care Male 482 58 43.25 - 68
Austria Met Health Care Female 507 53 38 - 65
Austria Unmet Health Care Male 17 57 44 - 66
Austria Unmet Health Care Female 32 56.5 36 - 73.25
Belgium Met Health Care Male 646 55 40 - 66
Belgium Met Health Care Female 609 52 37 - 64
Belgium Unmet Health Care Male 20 53.5 38 - 62.25
Belgium Unmet Health Care Female 19 47 36 - 56.5
Czechia Met Health Care Male 341 57.5 45 - 70
Czechia Met Health Care Female 518 53 41 - 68
Czechia Unmet Health Care Male 29 52 41 - 60
Czechia Unmet Health Care Female 50 49.5 38 - 62.75
Finland Met Health Care Male 788 57 42 - 69.25
Finland Met Health Care Female 864 55 39 - 66
Finland Unmet Health Care Male 66 59.5 45 - 71
Finland Unmet Health Care Female 99 55 39 - 69
France Met Health Care Male 513 54 41 - 65
France Met Health Care Female 604 48 36.75 - 61
France Unmet Health Care Male 43 49 35 - 58
France Unmet Health Care Female 49 53 37 - 63
United Kingdom Met Health Care Male 558 59 46 - 71
United Kingdom Met Health Care Female 628 57 42 - 70
United Kingdom Unmet Health Care Male 73 54 45.75 - 71
United Kingdom Unmet Health Care Female 111 56 42 - 67
Hungary Met Health Care Male 288 54 39.5 - 66
Hungary Met Health Care Female 405 53 41 - 66
Hungary Unmet Health Care Male 20 50 41.5 - 61.75
Hungary Unmet Health Care Female 44 46.5 31.75 - 59.25
Iceland Met Health Care Male 495 56 41 - 69
Iceland Met Health Care Female 563 54 41 - 65
Iceland Unmet Health Care Male 40 50 36 - 59.75
Iceland Unmet Health Care Female 43 42 31 - 52
Poland Met Health Care Male 278 53 40 - 66
Poland Met Health Care Female 284 45 31.5 - 59
Poland Unmet Health Care Male 39 57 36 - 67
Poland Unmet Health Care Female 44 49.5 36.5 - 68.25
Portugal Met Health Care Male 292 52 41 - 65
Portugal Met Health Care Female 386 48 37 - 58
Portugal Unmet Health Care Male 28 46 39 - 56
Portugal Unmet Health Care Female 73 49 37 - 57
Slovenia Met Health Care Male 459 52 36 - 64
Slovenia Met Health Care Female 584 46 34 - 60
Slovenia Unmet Health Care Male 49 40 28 - 52
Slovenia Unmet Health Care Female 59 48 34.5 - 59.5

Across most countries, the median ages for respondents with met and unmet healthcare needs are broadly similar, though some notable differences emerge.

  • Ireland: unmet males = 59.5, met males = 57
  • Austria: unmet females = 56.5, met females = 53
  • Poland: unmet females = 49.5, met females = 45

This suggests that in some countries, those reporting unmet healthcare needs tend to be slightly older. However, this is not universal.

  • Iceland: unmet females = 42 vs met females = 54
  • Slovenia: unmet males = 40 vs met males = 52

Across nearly all countries, female respondents generally have slightly lower median ages than males within the same healthcare need group. This pattern is visible in both the boxplot medians and the table.

The interquartile ranges (age_range) show that age dispersion differs across countries.

  • Austria females unmet: 36–73.25 — wide spread
  • Poland females unmet: 36.5–68.25 — wide spread
  • Iceland females unmet: 31–52 — much narrower

\[ \begin{aligned} \log \left( \frac{P(\text{Unmet Health Care Need})} {1 - P(\text{Unmet Health Care Need})} \right) &= \beta_0 + \beta_1(\text{Age}) + \beta_2(\text{Gender}) + \beta_3(\text{Country}) \\ &\quad + \beta_4(\text{Subjective Mental Health}) + \beta_5(\text{Subjective Physical Health}) \\ &\quad + \beta_6(\text{Loneliness}) + \beta_7(\text{Social Support}) \\ &\quad + \beta_8(\text{Currently in Paid Work}) \\ &\quad + \beta_9(\text{Early-Life Financial Difficulty}) \\ &\quad + \beta_{10}(\text{Healthcare Costs Covered}) \end{aligned} \]

model_age=glm(
  needed_any ~ age + gndr + cntry +
    w3hq57 + w3hq58 +w5hq12 + w5hq11 +w3hq77+w5hq8 + w5hq18 ,
  family = binomial,
  data = primary_health_wave
)

summary(model_age)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = needed_any ~ age + gndr + cntry + w3hq57 + w3hq58 + 
##     w5hq12 + w5hq11 + w3hq77 + w5hq8 + w5hq18, family = binomial, 
##     data = primary_health_wave)
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept) -3.859300   0.365779 -10.551  < 2e-16 ***
## age         -0.006389   0.003125  -2.044  0.04093 *  
## gndrFemale   0.133281   0.082074   1.624  0.10439    
## cntryBE     -0.601424   0.288038  -2.088  0.03680 *  
## cntryCZ      0.475398   0.228559   2.080  0.03753 *  
## cntryFI      0.631022   0.202650   3.114  0.00185 ** 
## cntryFR      0.515273   0.220816   2.333  0.01962 *  
## cntryGB      1.047240   0.202353   5.175 2.28e-07 ***
## cntryHU      0.305045   0.236269   1.291  0.19667    
## cntryIS      0.540062   0.226166   2.388  0.01695 *  
## cntryPL      0.882799   0.224310   3.936 8.30e-05 ***
## cntryPT      0.891963   0.221169   4.033 5.51e-05 ***
## cntrySI      0.643099   0.211319   3.043  0.00234 ** 
## w3hq57       0.046324   0.055367   0.837  0.40277    
## w3hq58       0.169747   0.055339   3.067  0.00216 ** 
## w5hq12       0.251227   0.059956   4.190 2.79e-05 ***
## w5hq11      -0.003727   0.016807  -0.222  0.82449    
## w3hq77       0.082788   0.099898   0.829  0.40726    
## w5hq8       -0.166274   0.037668  -4.414 1.01e-05 ***
## w5hq18       0.290683   0.035416   8.208 2.26e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 4903.1  on 8159  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 4568.2  on 8140  degrees of freedom
##   (3987 observations deleted due to missingness)
## AIC: 4608.2
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6

Based on the output we can say that

formula used

\[ OR = e^{\beta} \]

  • As the estimate of age is negative each additional year is associated with 0.6% lower odds of unmet healthcare need and is significantly associated with unmet healthcare need

  • Although females show slightly higher estimated odds, this difference is not statistically significant

  • Substantial cross-country differences were observed in the odds of reporting unmet healthcare need relative to Austria. Belgium exhibited significantly lower odds, while most other countries showed significantly higher odds. Particularly large effects were observed for the United Kingdom, Poland, and Portugal, where respondents had more than twice the odds of reporting unmet healthcare need compared with Austria. Hungary showed higher estimated odds; however, this difference was not statistically significant.

  • Worse subjective mental health was associated with slightly higher estimated odds of reporting unmet healthcare need (approximately 5% per one-level increase); however, this relationship was not statistically significant.

  • Worse subjective physical health was significantly associated with unmet healthcare need. Specifically, each one-level worsening in physical health was associated with approximately 19% higher odds of reporting unmet healthcare need.next

  • Higher levels of loneliness were significantly associated with unmet healthcare need. Specifically, each one-level increase in loneliness was associated with approximately 29% higher odds of reporting unmet healthcare need.

  • Greater social support from close contacts was associated with a very small decrease in the estimated odds of unmet healthcare need; however, this relationship was not statistically significant.

  • Being in paid work was associated with approximately 9% higher estimated odds of reporting unmet healthcare need; however, this relationship was not statistically significant.

  • Lower levels of childhood financial difficulty were significantly associated with reduced odds of reporting unmet healthcare need. Specifically, each one-level movement toward less frequent financial hardship during the first 18 years of life was associated with approximately 15% lower odds of unmet healthcare need.

  • Poorer perceived healthcare cost coverage was strongly and significantly associated with unmet healthcare need. Specifically, each one-level movement toward disagreement that healthcare costs are covered by the system was associated with approximately 34% higher odds of reporting unmet healthcare need.

Drawbacks

  • ordinal variables were not at level we are alsoing having gender baseline cntry basline and so on
primary_health_wave$w3hq77=factor(
  primary_health_wave$w3hq77,
  levels = c(1,2),
  labels = c("Yes","No")
)
primary_health_wave$w3hq57=factor(
  primary_health_wave$w3hq57,
  levels=c(5,4,3,2,1),
  labels=c("Very bad","Bad","Fair","Good","Very good"),
  ordered=TRUE
)
primary_health_wave$w3hq58=factor(
  primary_health_wave$w3hq58,
  levels=c(5,4,3,2,1),
  labels=c("Very bad","Bad","Fair","Good","Very good"),
  ordered=TRUE
)
primary_health_wave$w5hq8=factor(
  primary_health_wave$w5hq8,
  levels=c(5,4,3,2,1),
  labels=c("Never","Hardly ever","Sometimes","Often","Always"),
  ordered=TRUE
)
primary_health_wave$w5hq12=factor(
  primary_health_wave$w5hq12,
  levels = c(1,2,3,4),
  labels = c(
    "None or almost none of the time",
    "Some of the time",
    "Most of the time",
    "All or almost all of the time"
  ),
  ordered = TRUE
)
primary_health_wave$w5hq18=factor(
  primary_health_wave$w5hq18,
  levels = c(5,4,3,2,1),
  labels = c(
    "Disagree strongly",
    "Disagree",
    "Neither agree nor disagree",
    "Agree",
    "Agree strongly"
  ),
  ordered = TRUE
)
model_age=glm(
  needed_any ~ age + gndr + cntry +
    w3hq57 + w3hq58 +w5hq12 + w5hq11 +w3hq77+w5hq8 + w5hq18 ,
  family = binomial,
  data = primary_health_wave
)

summary(model_age)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = needed_any ~ age + gndr + cntry + w3hq57 + w3hq58 + 
##     w5hq12 + w5hq11 + w3hq77 + w5hq8 + w5hq18, family = binomial, 
##     data = primary_health_wave)
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept) -1.990421   0.268916  -7.402 1.35e-13 ***
## age         -0.005880   0.003157  -1.862  0.06257 .  
## gndrFemale   0.134257   0.082534   1.627  0.10381    
## cntryBE     -0.592660   0.289418  -2.048  0.04058 *  
## cntryCZ      0.486692   0.230212   2.114  0.03451 *  
## cntryFI      0.634255   0.204055   3.108  0.00188 ** 
## cntryFR      0.535979   0.222142   2.413  0.01583 *  
## cntryGB      1.041521   0.203672   5.114 3.16e-07 ***
## cntryHU      0.304775   0.238351   1.279  0.20101    
## cntryIS      0.533488   0.227486   2.345  0.01902 *  
## cntryPL      0.883529   0.226256   3.905 9.42e-05 ***
## cntryPT      0.887811   0.223165   3.978 6.94e-05 ***
## cntrySI      0.665298   0.212887   3.125  0.00178 ** 
## w3hq57.L    -0.355767   0.244064  -1.458  0.14493    
## w3hq57.Q     0.172832   0.193569   0.893  0.37193    
## w3hq57.C     0.035153   0.146668   0.240  0.81058    
## w3hq57^4    -0.140851   0.102866  -1.369  0.17092    
## w3hq58.L    -0.687212   0.225714  -3.045  0.00233 ** 
## w3hq58.Q     0.163281   0.183325   0.891  0.37311    
## w3hq58.C    -0.158196   0.137318  -1.152  0.24930    
## w3hq58^4     0.120173   0.091957   1.307  0.19127    
## w5hq12.L     0.665828   0.167033   3.986 6.71e-05 ***
## w5hq12.Q     0.244732   0.138192   1.771  0.07657 .  
## w5hq12.C     0.271517   0.119145   2.279  0.02267 *  
## w5hq11      -0.004061   0.016842  -0.241  0.80946    
## w3hq77No     0.071982   0.100545   0.716  0.47404    
## w5hq8.L      0.560599   0.130447   4.298 1.73e-05 ***
## w5hq8.Q      0.093609   0.113319   0.826  0.40877    
## w5hq8.C      0.053393   0.098235   0.544  0.58677    
## w5hq8^4      0.053446   0.079917   0.669  0.50364    
## w5hq18.L    -0.855701   0.114396  -7.480 7.42e-14 ***
## w5hq18.Q     0.112078   0.101274   1.107  0.26843    
## w5hq18.C     0.185212   0.088625   2.090  0.03663 *  
## w5hq18^4     0.196605   0.081047   2.426  0.01527 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 4903.1  on 8159  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 4543.6  on 8126  degrees of freedom
##   (3987 observations deleted due to missingness)
## AIC: 4611.6
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6

  • There is weak evidence suggesting that age may be negatively associated with unment health care but the effect is not statistically significant at the 5% level

  • Females have about 14% higher odds of having unmet healthcare needs compared to males, holding all other variables constant but the effect is statistically significant at 5% singificanat level

  • Country of residence shows substantial variation in unmet healthcare needs. Compared to the reference AT (Austria), individuals in several countries—such as GB(United Kingdom), PL(Poland), PT(Portugal), FI(Finland), FR(France), and SI(Slovenia) —have significantly higher odds of reporting unmet healthcare needs. For example, individuals in GB have nearly 2.8 times higher odds compared to those in AT.In contrast, individuals in BE have significantly lower odds of unmet healthcare needs relative to AT. Some countries (e.g., HU(Hungary)) do not show statistically significant differences from the reference category, indicating that their levels of unmet healthcare needs are not distinguishable from AT based on this model.

  • w3hq58 There is a significant negative linear relationship between Subjective physical health in general and unmet health care

  • w5hq12There is a significant positive linear relationship between Felt lonely during the past week parameter and unmet health care

  • w5hq8 There is a significant positive linear relationship between financial difficulties bellow the age of 18 parameter and unmet health care

  • w5hq18 Healthcare costs covered by system and unmet healthcare shows a strong and statistically significant negative linear association In addition, significant higher-order (cubic and quadratic) terms suggest that the relationship is not strictly linear, indicating some complexity in how different levels of the variable relate to the outcome

model_2 =glmer(
  needed_any ~ age + gndr + w5hq18 + (1 | cntry),
  family = binomial,
  data = primary_health_wave
)
summary(model_2)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: needed_any ~ age + gndr + w5hq18 + (1 | cntry)
##    Data: primary_health_wave
## 
##       AIC       BIC    logLik -2*log(L)  df.resid 
##    5359.9    5417.0   -2672.0    5343.9      9264 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -0.6337 -0.3507 -0.2694 -0.2241  6.8928 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  cntry  (Intercept) 0.1612   0.4015  
## Number of obs: 9272, groups:  cntry, 11
## 
## Fixed effects:
##              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept) -2.068605   0.182972 -11.306   <2e-16 ***
## age         -0.005829   0.002315  -2.518   0.0118 *  
## gndrFemale   0.120289   0.075499   1.593   0.1111    
## w5hq18.L    -0.989193   0.104351  -9.479   <2e-16 ***
## w5hq18.Q     0.085424   0.093304   0.916   0.3599    
## w5hq18.C     0.186397   0.081632   2.283   0.0224 *  
## w5hq18^4     0.160035   0.074633   2.144   0.0320 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##            (Intr) age    gndrFm w518.L w518.Q w518.C
## age        -0.673                                   
## gndrFemale -0.289  0.076                            
## w5hq18.L    0.041 -0.074  0.066                     
## w5hq18.Q    0.061  0.004  0.026  0.001              
## w5hq18.C    0.011 -0.013 -0.016  0.256  0.025       
## w5hq18^4   -0.011  0.043  0.002 -0.014  0.120  0.031

before adding w5hq12 (Felt lonely during the past week) we are going to check correlation of w5hq12 and w5hq18

cor(as.numeric(primary_health_wave$w5hq18),
    as.numeric(primary_health_wave$w5hq12),
    method = "spearman",
    use = "complete.obs")
## [1] -0.1180322

w5hq18 and w5hq12 are not strongly correlated, so they are unlikely to cause multicollinearity issues in the model. we used spearman method here because we wanted to take based on their rank in ordeinal rather than thier actual values

model_3=glmer(
  needed_any~age+gndr+w5hq18+w5hq12+(1|cntry),
  family=binomial,
  data=primary_health_wave
)
summary(model_3)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]

will be checking cor again with w5hq8 with the other paremters in the model

cor(as.numeric(primary_health_wave$w5hq8),
    as.numeric(primary_health_wave$w5hq18),
    method = "spearman",
    use = "complete.obs")
## [1] -0.135752
cor(as.numeric(primary_health_wave$w5hq8),
    as.numeric(primary_health_wave$w5hq12),
    method = "spearman",
    use = "complete.obs")
## [1] 0.1735201

its safe to add w5hq8 after checking correlation of the variables

model_4=glmer(
  needed_any~age+gndr+w5hq18+w5hq12+w5hq8+(1|cntry),
  family=binomial,
  data=primary_health_wave
)
summary(model_4)
